title: Accessibility Testing Tools
summary: Accessibility testing tools help you find problems automatically.
Browser extensions and audits can scan a page.
They catch many issues but not all.
Some checks still need a human.

sections.text: You do not have to find every accessibility problem by hand. A range of tools can scan your page and flag issues automatically, giving you a fast first pass. But it is important to know what they can and cannot catch.

sections.title: What the Tools Do
sections.text: Accessibility tools, including browser extensions and the audit built into Lighthouse, scan your page against known rules. They flag things like missing alt text, low contrast, or form fields without labels, and point you right to the problem. They turn a careful manual review into a quick automatic scan.

sections.title: What They Cannot Catch
sections.text: These tools are a great start, but they cannot judge everything. They can tell if an image has alt text, but not whether that text is meaningful. They cannot fully test whether your page makes sense to a screen reader user, or flows logically by keyboard. Some checks still need a human, ideally including real users.

media.alt: A tool flagging clear issues while a person reviews the rest

sections.title: A Spell Checker, Not an Editor
sections.text: Accessibility tools are like a spell checker. They reliably catch clear, mechanical mistakes, a missing label, a bad contrast. But just as a spell checker cannot tell if your sentence makes sense, these tools cannot tell if your page truly works for a real person. You still need human judgment on top.

media.alt: A spell checker catching typos but missing whether the meaning makes sense

sections.title: Testing Accessibility
items: Run an automatic tool, like a browser extension or Lighthouse.
Fix the clear issues it flags.
Try it with a screen reader.
Where possible, get feedback from real users.

sections.text: Studies have found that automatic tools catch only a portion of accessibility issues on their own. They are essential, but the rest comes from testing the way real people actually use the page.

terms.definition: Software that scans for accessibility issues.
Checking a page against known rules.
Found by the tool without a person.
A tool with a built in accessibility audit.

questions.text: What do accessibility testing tools do?
questions.explanation: Accessibility testing tools find many problems automatically.
Browser extensions and audits like Lighthouse can scan a page.

options: No, some checks still need a human
